title: Teenager drops social media addiction lawsuit against Meta
description: A Florida teenager who sued Meta claiming that he was addicted to and harmed by his use of platforms, such as Instagram, has dropped his case.The 15-y
body: A Florida teenager who sued Meta claiming that he was addicted to and harmed by his use of platforms, such as Instagram, has dropped his case.The 15-year-old plaintiff, known by the initials R.K.C., had in recent weeks settled the same claims against TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube, all of which have been accused in a large lawsuit of creating addictive platforms harmful to children.R.K.C.'s claims were to go before a Los Angeles jury next week as part of court proceedings that have seen Meta and YouTube lose once already.A Meta spokeswoman told the BBC: "The claims never held up, and this outcome makes clear that we will not back away from defending ourselves against baseless lawsuits."Lawyers for R.K.C., Emily Jeffcott and Rahul Ravipudi, told the BBC that the young plaintiff "came into this process wanting to hold social media companies accountable and push for changes to protect young people like himself". "He did that," they added.The lawyers noted concerns about a jury trial stretching on for weeks and said R.K.C. was "ready to close this chapter and focus on his recovery and engage in therapy as he aspires to have a normal life".The Meta spokeswoman said he received no payment for dropping his claims. R.K.C. had claimed that common social media features such as infinite scroll and autoplay, both of which continuously and automatically show users new content on a platform, drove compulsive use that became a type of addiction. He argued it caused him anxiety and sleep deprivation, among other issues.Meta and YouTube faced similar accusations from a young woman during the initial trial.A jury ultimately found them liable for harming her mental health and were told to pay her $6mn (£4.4mn) in damages.It was the first time that social media platforms had been held liable for their effects on users.Both companies have now appealed that verdict. The Los Angeles Superior Court is overseeing hundreds of lawsuits making similar claims of addiction and harm against social media companies. A small number of cases have been selected to go to trial.The next one is set to begin in October, according to court records.Across the US, there are thousands of lawsuits accusing Meta and other major social media platforms of harming young children.In May, Meta settled for an undisclosed amount with a school district that had sued it for negatively impacting students, so much so that schools incurred costs related to student welfare. The school district had sought $60m in damages.That settlement came not long after a New Mexico judge told Meta to pay $375m for misleading users on the safety of its platforms for children.

schema.headline: Teenager drops social media addiction lawsuit against Meta

title: Natalie Fleet leaves 'triggering' safeguarding minister role
description: Natalie Fleet says she has stepped down as safeguarding minister after finding the role "overwhelmingly triggering", telling the BBC that it was makin
body: Natalie Fleet says she has stepped down as safeguarding minister after finding the role "overwhelmingly triggering", telling the BBC that it was making her ill.The Labour MP, who was groomed and raped as a teenager, added she told the team of new Prime Minister Andy Burnham that she "sadly couldn't stay" in the job.Fleet described it as the "bravest thing I've ever done" and offered her support to her successor.She was appointed to the role on 12 May this year following the resignation of Jess Phillips, who said opportunities for progress had been "stalled and delayed" under the leadership of then-Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer.Announcing her departure on the social media platform X, external, Fleet said: "Honour of lifetime to be minister for safeguarding. Quickly found contents overwhelmingly triggering."She added: "Victims deserve someone at their best."She told BBC News the nature of the role as safeguarding minister means "you hear the worst of humanity", saying: "I understand what it is like not to be seen and not to be heard.""I know how important this job is and I know how much it needs a good and competent minister. It quickly became apparent how ill it was making me."She received support from the Home Office while in post, she said."But even with that [support] the nature of the job means that you hear the worst of humanity."Anybody would struggle hearing that but not everyone would react in the same way as someone who has gone through what I have."For me it is one of the most important jobs in government. It was the honour of my life to be able to do it. "Nothing can compare to being asked to make a difference to the lives of women and girls. It was incredible."Fleet was elected MP for Bolsover in July 2024, succeeding Conservative Mark Fletcher. From 1970 until 2019, the seat was held by Labour stalwart Dennis Skinner.She has spoken openly about her experiences as a teenager, including in an interview with GB News in 2024.Fleet said she became pregnant at the age of 15 after being groomed and raped by an older man, adding she has "weekly nightmares" about what she said happened to her."I didn't know we were having unprotected sex," she told the broadcaster. "I was a child and (it) is statutory rape."The MP said she fell pregnant and gave birth to her daughter, who she described as the "absolute love of my life".In her maiden speech in the House of Commons in October 2024, Fleet said it was her "duty" to speak up for women in her Derbyshire constituency "whose stories are not being told".

schema.headline: Natalie Fleet leaves 'triggering' safeguarding minister role

title: Isabel Rose: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong
description: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of j
body: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped.Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents.At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment.In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint."Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex."He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better."In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick."The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully."He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000.Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone."In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that."Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

schema.headline: Isabel Rose: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong

title: Casemiro: Inter Miami being investigated for tampering after signing ex-Manchester United midfielder
description: Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.On Wednesday, Mi
body: Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.On Wednesday, Miami announced they had signed the 34-year-old - who left Manchester United at the end of last season - on a deal that runs to the end of the 2027 MLS campaign, with an option to extend until June 2029.But moments later, the MLS released a statement saying it was "reviewing a tampering allegation" against the club."The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete," it added."While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the discovery priority [first rights] to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation."The investigation concerns Casemiro's discovery rights, an MLS regulation that gives a specific club first refusal over a player should they want to join.A club may have up to five players on their discovery list at any time and can add or remove players.Los Angeles Galaxy held the discovery rights for Casemiro but in announcing the player's signing, Inter Miami said they had acquired the rights from the Galaxy.LA Galaxy said in a statement: "The LA Galaxy and Inter Miami CF have reached a settlement for the discovery priority to sign Casemiro, and the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation currently open with Major League Soccer."Casemiro announced in January that he would depart Old Trafford at the end of the 2026-27 season following a mutual decision not to renew his contract.He won five Champions League titles with Real Madrid before moving to United in 2022, who played for Brazil as they reached the last 16 at this summer's World Cup."What motivates me the most, and I think this is true for every player, is winning and continuing to grow," he said.Casemiro made 160 appearances for United, scoring 26 goals and providing 14 assists as he won one EFL Cup and one FA Cup.In the wake of Casemiro's departure, United have prioritised midfield reinforcements in this window, buying Andrey Santos for £50m from Chelsea, and Youri Tielemans from Aston Villa for £35m.Before the announcement of the investigation, Inter Miami co-owner Sir David Beckham said of Casemiro: "He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I'm delighted that he's decided to make Miami his next home."At Miami, Casemiro is set to join former Real and Manchester United team-mate Sergio Reguilon as well as Argentina's World Cup finalists Lionel Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.It is not the first time Inter Miami have been probed over transfer dealings.In 2021 they were fined $2m after breaking MLS rules with the signing of France midfielder Blaise Matuidi from Juventus the previous year.The MLS said Matuidi had been paid more than was allowed, effectively giving the team four "designated players" last season rather than the permitted three. Designated players' wages do not count towards the MLS salary cap.Known as the 'Beckham rule', it was introduced when the ex-England captain joined Los Angeles Galaxy in 2007.

schema.headline: Casemiro: Inter Miami being investigated for tampering after signing ex-Manchester United midfielder

title: PC Harper's widow criticises early prisoner release plan as Burnham to review scheme
description: The widow of PC Andrew Harper has described the possible early release of prisoners as "deplorable", following reports that two of his killers could b
body: The widow of PC Andrew Harper has described the possible early release of prisoners as "deplorable", following reports that two of his killers could be freed early.Lissie Harper said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims.Prime Minister Andy Burnham earlier said he would review the Sentencing Act policy that from September would see thousands of offenders in England and Wales eligible to be released earlier.Speaking on Wednesday, Burnham said: "I can't say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward."He acknowledged that there was "pressure on prisons places" and that a failure to invest in the prison system in the past had created "an unacceptable situation".Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but could be released early under the new Sentencing Act.PC Harper was killed after he was dragged to his death behind their getaway car. The car's driver, Henry Long, was jailed for 16 years for manslaughter but would not be eligible for early release.Burnham's comments came after the mother of police officer Andrew Harper said the potential early release of two of her son's killers felt like an "insult".In a separate statement released later on Wednesday, PC Harper's widow Lissie Harper said: "It has taken me years to rebuild a life that was torn apart in a matter of moments. Years to pick up the pieces left behind by three men whose actions robbed Andrew of his life and stole our future."It is deplorable to me that we have reached a point where releasing prisoners early is even considered acceptable. We speak of justice, accountability and standing with victims, yet decisions like this do the opposite."The overcrowding of the prison estate has been a problem for several years and successive governments have released prisoners early in a bid to free up space.Tens of thousands of inmates have been let out of prison to create more space in jails since 2024.The Sentencing Act, which was passed by Sir Keir Starmer's government earlier this year, was designed to ease pressure on prisons, and will see some offenders spend less time in prison before release.Burnham said: "I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary."Concerning the case of PC Harper, he said: "I feel for the family it's an appalling crime that was committed."He said the "main perpetrator" of his death would not be eligible for early release.Burnham also said it was not acceptable to joke about early releases and that it was right Defence Secretary Wes Streeting had apologised for comments he made that were caught on camera.The prison population in England and Wales has risen steeply since the mid-1990s, doubling in size between 1992 and 2012.Shortly after coming into power, the Labour government, faced with warnings that prisons would run out of space within days, released thousands of inmates.The Sentencing Act reduces the point of release meaning some prisoners could be let out after serving a third of their sentence, while those who would previously have served two-thirds will instead be released after serving half of their sentence.The legislation also says courts should stop handing out short prison sentences of under a year, other than in specific or exceptional circumstances. Katie Kempen, chief executive at Victim Support, said: "It's encouraging to hear the new PM will be looking again at the early release policy. "This review must urgently fix the fact that most victims currently have no way of knowing whether or not an offender could be released early."Conservative shadow justice minister Nick Timothy said: "Labour's early release scheme has been catastrophic. Victims should not have to live in fear all summer waiting for a review."It would be shameful if the killers of PC Andrew Harper, grooming gang perpetrators and rapists were to get out of prison early, but this is what Labour had planned."Thames Valley Police Chief Constable Jason Hogg said he had written to the government to make it clear he believed the early release of Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers would be wrong.There are just under 3,000 vacant spaces in prisons across England and Wales, more than this time two years ago when they were on the verge of reaching full capacity. The Sentencing Act was created to help alleviate some of that pressure by letting out more than 5,000 inmates earlier than their potential release date so that more spaces would be created and staff weren't overstretched.If Burnham were to pull the plans, prison staff have told the BBC it could be "highly problematic". They had been expecting thousands to be released in the autumn under the new law - freeing up cells for people handed custodial sentences. One senior prison staffer said "with such little headroom, we need more cells to put people in. Otherwise it's going to get hairy again. We don't want a repeat of 2024."

schema.headline: PC Harper's widow criticises early prisoner release plan as Burnham to review scheme

title: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority
description: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authori
body: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who previously pledged to arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if he visited the city, now says he does not have the "authority" to do so.In a video posted on social media, Mamdani instead called on US officials to act on the International Criminal Court's arrest warrant for the Israeli Prime Minister over alleged war crimes. Technically, if Netanyahu set foot in an ICC member state, he could be arrested and handed over to the court - but the US is not a member of the ICC. President Donald Trump has said Netanyahu would not be "arrested, in any way, shape or form" while in the US."He is fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran, which recently killed 52,000 innocent protestors [sic], and has spent the last 47 years killing American Soldiers, and others," Trump said in a Truth Social post on Monday.Trump was responding to comments Mamdani made in an interview with the New York Times on Saturday, in which the mayor said he was having "an active conversation" with law enforcement about the possibility of enforcing the ICC arrest warrant.But on Tuesday, Mamdani said his team had reviewed "every avenue available" under the law, and concluded that "we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this"."The federal government, however, does," Mamdani stated. "And I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant."Mamdani said in the social media video that Netanyahu was "not welcome" in New  York City, where he is expected to travel in September for the UN General Assembly.The ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, while also issuing an arrest warrant for a Hamas commander.The ICC has the authority to prosecute those accused of genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes on the territory of states party to the Rome Statute, its founding treaty. During his campaign for New York City mayor, Mamdani repeatedly called Netanyahu a war criminal, and the war in Gaza a genocide. He also said he would order police to arrest Netanyahu if he arrived in the city.Speaking then to the New York Times as a candidate, he said: "This is something that I intend to fulfill." He said that such an arrest would be honouring a warrant issued by the ICC.His remarks come as Democratic voters' support for Israel appears to be shifting, with a recent poll , externalsuggesting 58% of Democrats believe the US is too supportive of Israel, up from 45% in 2024. In response to Mamdani's interview, Danny Danon, the Israeli ambassador to the UN said to the mayor: "You were elected to serve New Yorkers, not Hamas' propaganda."

schema.headline: Mamdani backs off  pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority

title: Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?
description: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Gen Oleksandr Syrskyi, 60, as Ukraine's commander-in-chief - the third since the start of Russia's full-sc
body: Maj Gen Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, has replaced Gen Oleksandr Syrskyi, 60, as Ukraine's commander-in-chief - the third since the start of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.His nomination has been welcomed by both civil society and the military. Drapatyi's name was chanted during protests that started off backing his friend, ex-defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, but ended up demanding Syrskyi's dismissal. "There's no better person for this position nowadays in Ukraine," one Ukrainian soldier told the BBC.Drapatyi's support rests on years of military experience in post-Soviet Ukraine, his skills as field general and his reputation for being a humane and strong commander among the military. He graduated from the Kharkiv Institute of Tank Troops in 2004 and began serving as a lieutenant.Ten years later he gained prominence when footage went viral showing his infantry fighting vehicle smashing through a makeshift barricade.It was May 2014 and pro-Russian separatists had taken over parts of eastern Ukraine, when Drapatiy's battalion entered the port of Mariupol to free hostages held in the city's police department. He later recalled being told there were barricades more than a metre high ahead and being asked what to do. "I said, 'let's take it by storm.' This video flew around the world," he said in an interview.Later that year, he was commended for withdrawing encircled Ukrainian units from a border area of the Luhansk region. Drapatiy returned to university in 2021 for further operational and strategic training and was presented with a sword of honour as top graduate in June 2021 by British ambassador Melinda Simmons.When Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022, Drapatiy was deputy commander of joint forces. He led operational groups - temporary task forces - in several areas of the front line, successfully pushing back the advance of Russian troops towards Kryvyi Rih and liberating a number of settlements in the Kherson and Dnipropretrovsk regions.By 2024 he was heading Ukraine's Ground Forces fighting in some of the east's most intense combat areas. Less than a year later, a training ground in the Dnipropetrovsk region was targeted by a Russian missile and 12 soldiers were killed and 60 others were wounded in the strike.Drapatyi handed in his resignation the very same day, saying he took personal responsibility for the "conspiracy of silence and impunity" that had led to the deaths."An army in which commanders bear personal responsibility for the lives of personnel keeps living. An army in which nobody is responsible for a loss dies," he wrote.It was seen as a sign of personal accountability, and Zelensky's decision to instead appoint him commander of the Joint Forces of the Armed Forces of Ukraine was widely praised. From then on, whispered rumours of tensions with Syrskyi grew louder. "Drapatyi is a relatively young general. He is gifted. He was always sent to places where the situation was extremely difficult," one unnamed officer told Ukrainian media in June 2025. "I do not think he has political ambitions. Yet I am sure his ambitions are not confined to the position of commander of the ground forces or the Joint Forces."Just over a year later, Syrskyi has gone, and hopes for Drapatyi are high. "Drapatyi has a very good reputation among the rank and file - especially those who joined after 2022 as civilians," says Mykola Bielieskov, senior analyst at the Ukrainian NGO Come Back Alive. But he notes Drapatyi will have to operate under serious constraints. "The commander-in-chief has a lot of leverage. But war is a combination of government, the president's office, military suppliers, dependence on [Western] partners... so he's got a tricky path before him." Prospects of a ceasefire with Russia remain slim and Ukraine faces another harsh winter of war.Mobilising manpower is a priority, and Drapatyi will also be expected to tackle the corruption that touches all levels of the army, from conscription officers to defence procurement. Meanwhile, he will have to capitalise on Ukraine's remarkable technological advances and the burgeoning tech defence industry that has spearheaded Ukraine's recent battlefield successes. In his first message in the new job, Drapatyi acknowledged the difficulty of his task and promised to work "without making loud promises, with responsibility for our decisions, and with respect" for those on the front line.Many will be looking for a change from what was seen as Syrskyi's "outdated" way of fighting, which placed little value on human life in favour of brutal offensives."It's a major role and people will expect change quickly," analyst Bielieskov says, warning against "oversimplification" that can lead to demand for sweeping improvements in an unrealistic length of time. This has been echoed by former interior ministry adviser Oleksiy Kopytko. As commander-in-chief, he noted, "you are worshipped and loved by all today, not loved by everyone tomorrow, and are guilty of everything the day after tomorrow, with none of your achievements mattering".Valery Zaluzhnyi, who was removed as Ukraine's army chief in 2024, predicted that Drapatyi would "have a very difficult time. Much harder than he imagines".But among many soldiers and ordinary Ukrainians there is a tangible sense of excitement for the new shift in outlook that Drapatyi represents. One said that if Fedorov were reinstated as defence minister, his and Drapatyi's leadership would be "Russia's worst nightmare". When he resigned from his position in 2025, Drapatyi listed issues he had tackled in the Ground Forces - ranging from combat training reform to unfair mobilisation, corruption and flawed procurement systems."There is expected resistance, but efforts need to be stepped up," he said at the time.He will face similar problems in his new role - albeit on a much larger scale and under the eyes of the whole country.Additional material by BBC Monitoring

schema.headline: Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new commander-in-chief?

title: Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat
description: ByThomas Copeland and Joshua CheethamBBC VerifyAt least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the Iran-linked Houthi group announce
body: ByThomas Copeland and Joshua CheethamBBC VerifyAt least seven oil tankers have made U-turns close to Yemen since the Iran-linked Houthi group announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia on Monday, ship-tracking data shows.All of the ships were travelling either to or from Saudi ports and made sharp changes of course after the embargo was announced.Nearly 15% of global sea trade moves through the Red Sea, which connects the Mediterranean and Gulf of Aden through two choke points - the Suez Canal in Egypt and the Bab al-Mandab Strait between Yemen and the Horn of Africa.The Red Sea has been a key route for exports of Saudi oil since the US-Israeli war with Iran led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has severely restricted the flow of oil and gas from the Gulf.This has played a "major role in relieving pressure to global markets" and if the Houthis further restrict trade then "prices will go up", said Rosemary Kelanic from the US-based Defense Priorities think tank.The day before the Houthis announced their blockade, 20 vessels entered the Red Sea and 22 exited via the Bab al-Mandeb strait, according to maritime intelligence firm Kpler.Kelanic told BBC Verify that any Houthi attacks on vessels in the Red Sea or Gulf of Aden "will suppress all international traffic not just Saudi-bound vessels".The EU's naval force in the region Aspides recommended on Wednesday that "merchant vessels linked to Israeli, US or Saudi interests avoid transiting the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden until the threat level decreases", according to an advisory seen by Reuters news agency.Ship-tracking data from MarineTraffic shows the crude oil tanker Rodos, loaded with Saudi crude, departed for India from the Red Sea port of al-Muajiz on Monday evening. The Liberian-flagged ship set off south towards the Bab al-Mandab Strait but about nine hours later made a U-turn and began sailing north instead.The Liberian-flagged oil and chemical tanker Mica departed Saudi Arabia's Jazan port on Monday afternoon and also headed south before reversing course.Ship-tracking data shows at least eight ships bound for the Red Sea have made U-turns instead of entering the Bab al-Mandab Strait from the south as well.Marshall Islands-flagged vehicle carrier Liu Jiang Kou was sailing to Saudi Arabia's Jeddah port from China before completely changing course on Monday in the Gulf of Aden.A Hong Kong-flagged crude oil tanker sailing from China called New Prime also turned around as it headed towards Saudi Arabia on Monday and now appears to be waiting in the Arabian Sea.While the reasons for each course change cannot be independently verified, "the timing is consistent with the embargo announcement," said maritime risk management company Vanguard.In an email to shipowners on Monday, which the BBC has seen, the Houthis said "vessels are banned from loading or discharging cargo at any Saudi ports". It added that ships "may be subject to targeting in any location within the operational reach".Image source, EPAImage caption, Houthi fighters during a protest against Saudi Arabia in Yemen's capital Sana'a on 17 JulyThe Houthis have said their embargo is in retaliation to a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Houthi-controlled north-western Yemen.Saudi Arabia has condemned the allegation and has vowed to "take all necessary measures to protect its ships in accordance with international law".Yemen has been devastated by a civil war since 2014, when the Houthis captured the capital of Sana'a from the government. The conflict escalated in 2015, after a Saudi-led coalition of Arab states intervened to try and restore the official government.There has been an informal truce between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia since 2022. But last week, the Houthis launched missiles at an airport in south-western Saudi Arabia in response to air strikes on Sanaa's airport that they blamed on the kingdom.The Houthis have previously caused severe disruption to the the 32km-wide (20-mile) Bab al-Mandab Strait.Shortly after the Gaza war broke out in October 2023, the Houthis began attacking merchant vessels in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, saying they were acting in support of the Palestinians. These attacks led to four ships being sunk, one vessel seized, and nine crew members killed.Since the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Saudi Arabia has diverted more than 70% of its crude exports from the Gulf to the Red Sea port of Yanbu through an east-west pipeline.About four million barrels per day have been shipped from Yanbu in recent weeks, compared with about 973,000 a year earlier, according to data from Kpler and maritime analytics platform Signal Ocean.Naveen Das, a senior oil analyst from Kpler, told BBC Verify the only option left to the Saudis if the Bab al-Mandab Strait is effectively closed would be to move oil all the way through the Mediterranean and around the southern tip of Africa to get to Asia.The impact of the blockade on consumers won't be immediate, but the conflict with Iran has made the global energy system more vulnerable to shocks like these, Das added. "We could weather it for two weeks, three weeks, even a month, but even in that time, we will see higher freight rates, higher energy prices and that bleeds into higher energy consumer costs."Ships close to the Red Sea are also broadcasting messages in an attempt to deter attacks, a common practise in the region since the Houthis targeted vessels during the war in Gaza.At least 50 vessels in the region are broadcasting they have armed guards on board, according to data from MarineTraffic.Martin Kelly, from crisis management firm EOS Risk Group, told BBC Verify that captains will fear the "Houthis might read your situation wrong" and think their ships have "called at a Saudi port in the past, then therefore you could be attacked"."A lot of these ships are very large crude oil carriers. I think the damage would be catastrophic. There would be certainly be deaths onboard these ships," he added.Additional reporting by Barbara Metzler

schema.headline: Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat

title: Ukrainian drones hit more sites of Russian online giant retailer Wildberries
description: Workers were evacuated after the attacks on logistics centres in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk, Wildberries founder and Russia's wealthiest woman Tatyana
body: Workers were evacuated after the attacks on logistics centres in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk, Wildberries founder and Russia's wealthiest woman Tatyana Kim said earlier on Wednesday."There are no words to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on us, who are simply doing our jobs," she wrote on Telegram. "But these situations demonstrate the most important thing: we must continue to do what we do for the benefit of people."In Krasnodar, 10 people were injured in an attack on a warehouse complex, with three in a serious condition, the region's Governor Veniamin Kondratyev said.He later said that a young woman died of her injuries in hospital.Five people were hurt in a warehouse complex on the outskirts of Nevinnomyssk, Stavropol Governor Vladimir Vladimirov said.BBC Verify has analysed footage and confirmed fires in the two locations.Several hours after the latest attacks, a range of military gear - including body armour and drone components - was widely available on Wildberries' website, a quick search by the BBC showed.Serhii Kuzan, chairman of the Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Centre, has described Wildberries as a "vital component" of Russian logistics, adding that the firm's website is being used by Russian volunteers to buy military equipment."The primary rationale for striking Wildberries' warehouses is to disrupt Russian logistics and the supply of dual-use goods, critical electronics, sanctioned goods and the like to the Russian army and Russian arms manufacturers," Kuzan told the BBC."The collateral damage from such strikes could also have a serious impact on the Russian economy, as well as a psychological effect on Russian society and, likely, on continued support for the war."The Ukrainian strikes have also dealt a serious blow to Russia's small and mid-sized businesses, angering hundreds of sellers whose livelihoods depend on online marketplaces.Unlike retail giant Amazon, Wildberries and its rival Ozon act almost entirely as marketplaces, connecting independent sellers with buyers across Russia, storing and delivering their goods, and handling payments.The recent strikes have impacted hundreds of sellers whose livelihoods depend on online marketplaces.Trader Elena, not her real name, told BBC Russian she had lost clothing she sells in the attacks, which is her only income.She said that "if there are more strikes, I probably won't manage. It's terrifying to keep loading my goods into what feels like a furnace"."I am a mother of a disabled child, I've built the business myself", wrote another trader, in post that has gone viral on social media. "I've lost one million roubles (£9,500; $12,730) and it's just my problem now. I'm out of strength. We're already getting screwed over from every direction as it is," she wrote.Additional reporting by Paul Brown, BBC Verify, Vitaly Shevchenko, and BBC Russian

schema.headline: Ukrainian drones hit more sites of Russian online giant retailer Wildberries

title: Strait of Hormuz: Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if attacks on ship persist
description: President Donald Trump has threatened that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant any time its forces attack a ship in the Strait of Ho
body: President Donald Trump has threatened that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant any time its forces attack a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.Potential targets would include ones "located next to, or in," the capital Tehran, he said in a post on Truth Social.The military response would follow any form of attack on shipping in the vital waterway, "whether it be by missile, rocket, drone, or any other device or weapon".His threat follows the US launching strikes on Iran for the 11th consecutive night, with Tehran warning the US against targeting its nuclear facilities.Iranian state media reported that air defences were activated in the capital Tehran. Explosions were also reported in the north-western city of Tabriz, the south-eastern ports of Chabahar and Konarak, and in the southern city of Bushehr, home to Iran's only nuclear power plant.Iran, which has retaliated against the US and its Middle East allies, warned of reprisals after Trump said the US would soon hit an underground nuclear site near Natanz.Kuwait's army said on Wednesday that its air defences had engaged with drone attacks from Iran, while Bahrain and Jordan said they had also intercepted drone and missile strikes.The price of oil jumped in response to the latest escalation, reaching $94 on Wednesday. Iran's top negotiator, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, warned the US on Wednesday evening that "the equation of this war is clear: either all or none!"In a region where we do not sell oil, no-one will sell oil. If our security is not ensured, no infrastructure will be safe, and the security of the strait is in the absence of American forces. We have repeatedly said that the situation of the strait will not return to pre-war conditions," Ghalibaf wrote in a post on X.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio earlier said Washington was still willing to engage in diplomacy with Tehran, but added that he did not believe Iran was serious about talks. Prior to that, the US president said the next military target could be a complex known as Pickaxe Mountain, an underground nuclear site where Iran is suspected of building an undeclared enrichment facility.Trump also threatened to "take care of" the Houthi rebels if the Iran-backed militant group in Yemen followed through on its threatened blockade of Saudi ports.A Houthi media official had earlier said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.Following the Houthi announcement, two tankers which had just loaded Saudi crude bound for China and India at Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port of Yanbu made U-turns on Tuesday, heading instead towards the Suez Canal, British maritime risk management group Vanguard said. The threat from the Houthis risks further disruption to international shipping, with hostilities in the region having already led to global fuel prices fluctuating significantly.Iran and the US signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in June to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, as well as to reach an agreement to end the war over the following 60 days. But three weeks after it was signed, Trump declared the ceasefire "over" after Iranian attacks on ships in Hormuz, and US strikes in response.

schema.headline: Strait of Hormuz: Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if attacks on ship persist

title: Most bus fares in England to be capped at £2 from January
description: The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced.The government says the policy, which is exp
body: The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced.The government says the policy, which is expected to cost more than £500m, will "help with the cost of living and give people the breathing space they need".At the start of last year, the England-wide bus fare cap rose to £3 under Sir Keir Starmer's Labour government, although in Liverpool and Manchester fares were held at £2.Burnham said affordable transport links were an essential, adding: "Lower fares will help people get to where they need to."The measure gives people "a little bit of hope" in a time when many are grappling with higher living costs - especially after fuel costs spiked as a result of the US-Iran war, transport secretary Heidi Alexander told the BBC's Today programme.However, she admitted the measure was  "not the whole solution".The new cap will take effect from January next year for fares on participating buses outside of London. The cost of a non-discounted single bus journey in London is £1.75.The Burnham government said going back to a £2 cap should particularly help passengers in rural and coastal areas, where single fares can be higher.The Campaign for Better Transport said rural areas were still suffering from fewer bus routes and services, adding that such areas had been more affected by council cut backs in the last decade than urban zones.Services in those areas - as measured by vehicle miles - decreased by 18% between 2019 and 2024, according to the County Councils Network, which represents 39 unitary and county councils across England."What we need to see is the replacing of those lost services which have particularly affected those in rural areas," Campaign for Better Transport's Michael Solomon Williams said.Shadow Chancellor Mel Stride said the government has not actually specified where the money will come from to fund the bus fare cap extension."That is a worrying sign, we need to have a government which is on top of the public finances, not one that is making lots of spending commitments without explaining where the money is going to come from," Stride told BBC Breakfast.In its announcement, the government said £454m will be reallocated from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESN). DESN says this will be used in addition to funds already earmarked by the Department for Transport for buses.Alexander told the BBC it was money that had been allocated in DESN's budget to spend on international climate projects."And so instead of putting that money out as grants we are going to be issuing loans and so that money comes back in," she said.It is the second day in a row that the new prime minister has announced cost saving measures, following Tuesday's VAT cut on household electricity bills later this year.In his first cabinet meeting on Tuesday, Burnham promised to lead a "cost of living government", telling ministers that he wanted to give people a "sense that help is coming" on costs.

schema.headline: Most bus fares in England to be capped at £2 from January

title: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem
description: In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behin
body: In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behind Toy Story 5.In the process, The Odyssey broke the record for the highest takings in an opening weekend for a film on Imax screens in the UK, according to industry publication Screen Daily, external.The three-hour epic, which stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland and Anne Hathaway, was the first theatrical release to be shot entirely with Imax 70mm film cameras.While there are more than 50 Imax screens in the UK, only three are showing it using 70mm film projectors - the BFI and Science Museum in London, and Printworks in Manchester."You do feel like you're very close to the characters, you're immersed in the action. So it is a heightened experience, I would say," film critic and Girls on Film podcast host Anna Smith told the Today programme.Imax screenings are more expensive than regular cinema tickets, however."If you have the money and you've been lucky enough to book, I don't think you would regret seeing it in Imax 70mm," Metro film critic Tori Brazier said."But at the same time it's very expensive and we do live in a world where lots of people watch films on laptop screens and phone screens now, so if you're going to the cinema you're going to have a big-screen experience regardless."

schema.headline: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem

title: The Hundred 2026 results: Welsh Fire defeat Southern Brave with captain Salt starring
description: The Hundred men's competition, Utilita BowlSouthern Brave 135-7 (100 balls): Miller 44 (35); Jansen 2-17Welsh Fire 138-4 (98 balls): Salt 47* (37); Ch
body: The Hundred men's competition, Utilita BowlSouthern Brave 135-7 (100 balls): Miller 44 (35); Jansen 2-17Welsh Fire 138-4 (98 balls): Salt 47* (37); Chaudhary 2-19 Welsh Fire won by six wicketsScorecard. TableWelsh Fire captain Phil Salt led from the front as his unbeaten 47 helped them begin the 2026 Hundred season with a six-wicket win against Southern Brave in Southampton. Former world number one-ranked T20 batter Salt is The Hundred's all-time leading scorer, having hit 1,138 runs in five seasons with his former side Manchester Originals.Having signed for Fire as their captain before this year's competition, he is aiming to guide the Welsh franchise, who finished bottom of both the men's and women's table last season, to their first-ever title. Chasing 136 on Wednesday, Salt played the supporting role early on as fellow opener Matt Short struck a brisk 20 before he kept the visitors on course through partnerships with Joe Root (14) and Rachin Ravindra (24).With nine runs required from the final five balls, Asa Tribe hit Brave captain Chris Jordan's first ball for four before Salt sealed the victory with a six two balls later.Batting first, Brave lost openers Jamie Smith and Ben McKinney for single figures but 18-year-old Thomas Rew helped rebuild their innings with a flashy 41 from 30 balls.The Fire bowlers, led by Marco Jansen who finished with 2-17, kept the scoring in check for much of the innings.David Miller struggled to find his rhythm at the start of his innings but finished strongly for Brave before departing in the penultimate delivery with a 35-ball 44, which lifted the hosts to 135-7, which ultimately proved not enough.

schema.headline: The Hundred 2026 results: Welsh Fire defeat Southern Brave with captain Salt starring

title: Toue de France 2026: Why anti doping tests are being carried out at night
description: Drug testing at cycling's biggest race, the Tour de France is in the spotlight, following a series of night-time anti-doping tests on the sport's bigg
body: Drug testing at cycling's biggest race, the Tour de France is in the spotlight, following a series of night-time anti-doping tests on the sport's biggest names.Yellow jersey leader Tadej Pogacar from Slovenia and his main rival, Jonas Vingegaard, were tested in the middle of the night on Saturday.The Dane crashed out of the race on the following stage, with some believing his disrupted sleep could have been a factor. Five teams have reportedly had riders tested between 11pm and 6am, external during the 2026 edition of the race, this is outside the normal testing hours and usually a rare occurrence. BBC Sport understands there have to be "exceptional circumstances" to justify the effort and that, in these cases, time might have been of the essence with a need for prompt testing.Some in the paddock say the move is "effective" for a sport which needs to move away from a chequered doping past.Jonathan Vaughters, now boss of EF-Education Easypost and a former team-mate of the disgraced Lance Armstrong, has spoken in support of the nocturnal tests.He said on X: "Two am testing is one of the very effective ways to prevent peptide (EPO, HGH) and testosterone micro dosing. If [the] UCI had tested us in 2001 at 2am? We'd all have been suspended and saved the sport 25 years of drama."Others - including the cyclists' union the CPA - says waking riders in the middle of the night is potentially dangerous and unnecessary.But the sport's governing body, the UCI, (Union Cycliste Internationale) backed the night tests, stating the "fight against doping is an absolute priority".It added: "The UCI of course acknowledges that night-time testing can disrupt riders' rest and recovery, and wishes to reiterate that carrying out such controls outside normal hours remains an exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances."While fully aware of how onerous such a measure is for riders, the UCI nevertheless underscores that these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping."For the International Testing Agency - which is responsible for carrying out anti-doping tests on behalf of the UCI - to undertake tests in the middle of the night is rare, and described by many in the paddock as "unusual". Testing between 11pm and 6am is restricted in France, and the ITA must seek permission to do so - in this case from the Parisian public prosecutors' office. The specific reason has not been disclosed, and neither have the details of how riders were tested, or where. It is also not known when the results of the tests are to be released. On Monday, the second rest day of the Tour, all 166 remaining riders were tested, although these were carried out between 8pm and 11pm.Typically, the ITA will carry out about 600 tests across a three-week Grand Tour, in a process that often takes place after a stage and includes a rider having to give a urine sample while being observed by delegates through a window.To put it simply, the testing could help to combat micro-dosing. This is where tiny amounts of performance-enhancing drugs are ingested but are in and out of the rider's system within a few hours, leaving only a short window for detection.If a rider knows they have a seven-hour window where they are not normally tested, some believe it could be an opportunity to micro-dose. Vaughters said: "If you introduce a low enough dose of a peptide hormone, the clearance time can be four hours. If you take it at 11pm, it'll be out of the system at 3am, so it makes a difference." He added testing technology is evolving quickly but, where riders and teams push the boundaries, doping is always going to be a risk factor.On night testing, he added: "It sucks if it happened all the time - but it's happening once or twice a year. Listen, it's a great deterrent. One night of lost sleep; lose a little sleep - gain a lot of credibility."Generally, the days when riders boosted blood values using first EPO and then moved on to blood transfusions (which is why the night-time hotel room is such a taboo backdrop in cycling) are thought to have largely gone from the top of the sport. Since the 1990s and decade from 2000, when the disgraced once seven-time winner Armstrong tricked the world into thinking he was clean when dominating the sport, the athlete biological passport (ABP) used by Wada is sophisticated enough to pick up seemingly all inconsistencies recorded.There have been cyclists banned recently but no high-profile names.The sport's lower-tiers frequently has seen riders busted for being tempted back to EPO and human growth hormones.The ITA told BBC Sport: "Due consideration has been given to minimise as much as possible the impact testing activities have on riders."However, the ITA, on behalf of the UCI, has the responsibility to ensure the effectiveness of the cycling anti-doping program and the integrity of cycling races."As such, effective testing must, in limited and justified circumstances, be capable to occur outside standard daytime hours."The cyclists' union the CPA is not happy with the current system of testing riders and the potential dangers it puts cyclists in. Their president and former rider Adam Hansen said on X: "Our position is clear that the CPA is extremely disappointed with the current direction of anti-doping in professional cycling."Did you know that cyclists are the only athletes in the world who pay out of their own pockets to fund extra doping controls, long-term sample storage and for data analysis? "This financial burden is a direct consequence of our sport's historical past. While modern cycling is drastically cleaner today, active riders are paying for the mistakes of previous generations. Is that fair?"Within the SafeR initiative, members frequently claim that crashes happen because riders lose focus or look down at their bike computers. But consider the human cost of the current doping system, think about how you perform at your normal day job after only four hours of sleep, or after being woken up right in the middle of your REM [sleep] cycle at 2am for a blood test. "We have said, 'quality over quantity'. The riders are directly funding the ITA, and we have repeatedly argued that increasing more tests is not the solution."Instead, those funds should be invested in upgrading testing laboratory equipment capable of detecting the absolute smallest traces of banned substances. [This would mean] fewer but more precise tests that catch real cheaters while reducing constant harassment of clean riders."There are other methods on the horizon, however. The ITA is carrying out a feasibility study into using cyclists' power data as part of its anti-doping strategy.The Swiss group is working with five teams to gather data with the aim of supporting more traditional methods.There is some scepticism within cycling about whether such an approach will be of any additional benefit to a sport which has not had a major doping controversy for more than five years.But while lower-level riders are still being caught and the average speeds in the big races are creeping up, the issue never entirely goes away at a time when cycling, hurt by past scandals, looks to build and maintain a cleaner image.

schema.headline: Toue de France 2026: Why anti doping tests are being carried out at night

title: Ex-Southern Water boss charged over alleged plan to manipulate water tests
description: The ex-chief executive of Southern Water has been charged with three others over an alleged plan to manipulate water quality tests to avoid paying fin
body: The ex-chief executive of Southern Water has been charged with three others over an alleged plan to manipulate water quality tests to avoid paying financial penalties of about £45m, it can now be reported.Matthew Wright is accused of conspiring to defraud the Environment Agency (EA) and water regulator Ofwat between 2012 and 2017, along with Philip Barker, Clive Massey and Mark Gregory, who also worked at the firm.Wright's solicitor says he "denies all wrongdoing and has co-operated fully" with the EA's investigation.Southern Water said the charges related to "a historic set of failures" uncovered in an internal investigation. It said it was a "completely different" company to a decade ago.The EA asked a judge to issue a summons against the four, charging them with the offence, last year, but Wright launched a legal challenge, dismissed by two senior judges on Wednesday.Massey, Gregory and Barker did not appear at the hearing in London and were not represented.According to court documents, the allegations include arranging for wastewater to be removed from facilities by tankers in order to create "no flow" results.This had been "with a view to covering up pollution and deceiving those whose function is to protect the public from such harm", prosecutors allege.In his legal challenge, Wright's lawyers argued at the High Court in June that the EA did not have the power to issue the summons and it should be thrown out.But the judges dismissed his claim and ruled restrictions previously preventing reporting of the legal challenge should be lifted.Separate court listings showed the case against 60-year-old Wright, of Haslemere, Surrey; Barker, 57, of West Chiltington, West Sussex; Massey, 64, of Brandhill, Shropshire; and Gregory, 63, of Southampton, were scheduled to be heard at Medway Magistrates' Court on 14 July.The four are accused of conspiring between 2012 and 2017 to defraud those conducting the environmental and financial regulation duties of Southern Water, including the EA and Ofwat, by "the implementation of artificial no-flow events at wastewater treatment works".Court listings also show that Southern Water has separately been charged with dozens of offences of failing to comply with or contravening an environmental permit condition between 2013 and 2017 at multiple wastewater treatment works.This follows a previous Ofwat investigation, which resulted in a customer rebate and penalties being imposed in 2019.The Southern Water spokesperson added: "We apologised in full at that time and repeat that apology again today."Wastewater treatment works can only be run with an environmental permit issued by the EA.Treated effluent discharge from the works is tested under an "operator self-monitoring" (OSM) scheme, introduced in 2009, with discharges tested annually in unannounced visits.The tests are carried out by sampling teams, which operate separately from teams that run the works.The results of the tests are shared with the EA and Ofwat, and can lead to penalties if the works do not comply with the terms of their permits.The BBC has been analysing the pay of Matthew Wright between 2011 to 2016, and found that, in his final full year at Southern Water, he was paid £715,900.But, in the year prior to this, 2014-15, Wright earned close to £2m thanks to a one-off £1.25m payment as part of the water company's long-term incentive plan.This was in addition to a bonus of £226,000 in that same year.Alongside Wright, Barker, Massey and Gregory, three additional people have also been charged with failing to comply with or contravening an environmental permit condition.Terry Stephens, 68, of Waterlooville, Hampshire, is charged with one count, alleged to have taken place in 2015.David James, 60, of Worthing, West Sussex, is also charged with one count, alleged to have taken place in 2016.Mark Butler, 47, of Hassocks, West Sussex, is charged with eight counts.

schema.headline: Ex-Southern Water boss charged over alleged plan to manipulate water tests
